Summary: This study developed a combined model incorporating radiomics and CT imaging features to differentiate between malignant and benign subcentimeter solid pulmonary nodules (SSPNs). The model demonstrated excellent discriminative ability and clinical application value, potentially aiding radiologists in diagnosing SSPNs.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to any specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines like the CLEAR checklist.

Item2.reason: The study clearly defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient selection, ensuring a representative study population. The criteria include nodule size, confirmation of malignancy or benignity through pathology or follow-up, and image quality.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is well-defined, with malignant nodules confirmed by pathology and benign nodules confirmed by follow-up or pathology. The follow-up period of more than 2 years for benign nodules is appropriate. The median interval time between the CT scans and surgery was 16 (first quantile 9; third quantile 25) days.

Item4.reason: The study is a single-center retrospective study.

Item5.reason: The study uses contrast-enhanced CT, a standard imaging technique for pulmonary nodules. The imaging data source is clinically translatable.

Item6.reason: The CT protocol with acquisition parameters such as tube voltage, tube current, helical pitch, rotation time, slice thickness, and contrast injection details are provided.

Item7.reason: The interval between imaging and the reference standard (pathology or follow-up) is appropriate, with a median interval of 16 days between CT scans and surgery.

Condition1.reason: The study includes manual segmentation of the regions of interest (ROIs) of the nodules on CT images.

Condition2.reason: The segmentation was performed manually by a radiologist, not fully automated.

Item8.reason: The study describes the segmentation methodology, including the radiologist delineating the ROIs slice by slice while avoiding adjacent vessels, bronchi, and normal lung tissue.

Item9.reason: The segmentation was performed manually, not fully automated, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: The segmentation was performed by a single radiologist, reflecting clinical practice.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts hand-crafted radiomic features using the Pyradiomics package.

Item11.reason: The study mentions normalization and resampling during image pre-processing with a fixed bin width of 25, and all images were resampled to [1,1,0] after the B-spline interpolation sampling technology.

Item12.reason: The study used Deepwise Multimodal Research Platform version 2.0.1.4, which includes Pyradiomics (version 3.0.1) for feature extraction.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (first-order, shape, texture) and classes (GLCM, GLSZM, GLRLM, GLDM, NGTDM). It also mentions the three-level naming method for features.

Condition4.reason: The study includes tabular data in the form of extracted radiomic features and clinical/CT characteristics organized for analysis.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline. It uses hand-crafted radiomic features and logistic regression.

Item14.reason: The study assesses the interobserver reproducibility of the segmentation using intraclass correlation coefficients (ICCs) and includes features with ICC values > 0.75 in subsequent analysis.

Item15.reason: The study uses linear correlation coefficient analysis to remove redundant features and analysis of variance F-test for feature selection.

Item16.reason: The study does not explicitly discuss the appropriateness of dimensionality compared to data size or provide statistical justification for the number of instances and features used.

Item17.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline, so this item is not applicable.

Item18.reason: The study randomly divides the dataset into training and testing cohorts in a 7:3 ratio. It also uses fivefold cross validation in the training set.

Item19.reason: The study does not explicitly address the handling of potential confounding factors such as different distributions of patient characteristics across sites or scanners.

Item20.reason: The study reports appropriate accuracy metrics such as AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, and negative predictive value. ROC curves are also presented.

Item21.reason: The study reports 95% confidence intervals (CI) for the AUC values.

Item22.reason: The study does not include a calibration assessment of the final model.

Item23.reason: The study uses contrast-enhanced CT images, but it does not compare the performance with non-contrast-enhanced CT or justify the use of contrast enhancement through formal comparison.

Item24.reason: The study compares the performance of the combined model with clinical and radiomics models. The Delong test is used to compare the AUCs of different models. Decision curve analysis (DCA) was used to calculate the clinical impact of the models.

Item25.reason: The study does not compare the performance with a simple baseline reference model such as a Zero Rules/No Information Rate classifier.

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an independent data set (testing cohort) that is sampled from the same source as the training set.

Item27.reason: The study does not include external testing with independent data from other institutions.

Item28.reason: The study states that the datasets are not publicly available due to data sharing not obtaining the informed consent of the patients.

Item29.reason: The study does not provide information about the availability of the code used for segmentation and modeling.

Item30.reason: The study does not provide information about the availability of the final model.
